title: Separable Verbs — The Prefix That Flies to the End
description: German has verbs that split in two. The prefix detaches and shoots to the end of the sentence. Once you spot the pattern, sentences that looked scrambled suddenly make sense.

lessons.title: How Separable Verbs Split
lessons.subtitle: aufstehen → 'Ich stehe um 7 Uhr auf.' The prefix goes last.
lessons.hook: You look up 'aufstehen' (to get up), build a sentence, and… where did the 'auf' go? It flew to the very end: 'Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf.' This isn't a mistake — it's one of German's most systematic habits, and it's easy once you know the rule.
lessons.explain: Many German verbs have a separable prefix (auf-, an-, ein-, mit-, aus-, ab-, zu-, vor-, fern-). In a normal main-clause sentence, the verb splits: the main part is conjugated in position 2, and the prefix detaches and moves to the END. So aufstehen → 'Ich stehe … auf', anrufen → 'Ich rufe … an'. The prefix is often the meaningful part, so read all the way to the end before deciding what the verb means. You can usually hear the stress on the prefix, which is a clue that a verb is separable.

examples.german: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf.
examples.english: I get up at seven o'clock.

vocabulary.memoryHook: Separable: auf + stehen. 'Ich stehe … auf'. Change of state → sein in the perfect.
Separable: an + rufen. 'Ich rufe dich an.'
Separable: mit + kommen. 'Kommst du mit?' — a daily invitation.

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: In the evening I watch TV.

exercises.prompt: Where does the prefix of a separable verb go in a normal sentence?
options: to the end of the sentence
exercises.correctAnswer: to the end of the sentence
exercises.explanation: The conjugated part stays in position 2; the prefix detaches and moves to the end: 'Ich stehe um 7 Uhr auf.'

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Ich ___ meine Mutter an.' (I call my mother.) — verb: anrufen
exercises.hint: Conjugate 'rufen'; the 'an' is already at the end.
exercises.explanation: anrufen splits into 'rufe … an'. The conjugated 'rufe' goes to position 2 and 'an' stays at the end.
einkaufen splits: 'kaufe' in position 2, 'ein' at the very end: 'Ich kaufe am Samstag ein.'

lessons.title: Separable Verbs with Modals & in the Past
lessons.subtitle: With a modal they rejoin; in the Perfect the 'ge' sits inside.
lessons.hook: Good news: separable verbs only split in simple main clauses. Add a modal verb and they snap back together at the end. In the past tense, the 'ge-' even hides INSIDE the word. Two neat rules and you've got the whole system.
lessons.explain: Two important cases: (1) With a modal, the separable verb stays whole as an infinitive at the end — 'Ich muss früh aufstehen' (not 'stehe auf'). (2) In the Perfect tense, the past participle keeps the prefix and inserts -ge- between the prefix and the stem: aufstehen → aufgestanden, anrufen → angerufen, einkaufen → eingekauft. So: 'Ich habe meine Mutter angerufen', 'Ich bin früh aufgestanden'. The prefix never gets lost — it just travels or wraps around the 'ge'.

examples.english: I have to get up early.

breakdown.role: separable verb stays WHOLE at the end

vocabulary.memoryHook: aufstehen → aufgestanden (auf + ge + standen). Uses SEIN.
anrufen → angerufen (an + ge + rufen). Uses HABEN.
Separable: ab + fahren. Participle: abgefahren (uses sein).

exercises.prompt: With a modal verb, a separable verb…
exercises.explanation: A modal sends the main verb to the end as a full infinitive, so it does NOT split: 'Ich muss früh aufstehen.'

exercises.prompt: What is the Perfect participle of 'anrufen'?
exercises.explanation: Separable participles wrap the -ge- inside: an + ge + rufen = angerufen. 'Ich habe dich angerufen.'

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Ich bin früh ___.' (I got up early.) — verb: aufstehen
exercises.hint: auf + ge + standen; and aufstehen uses sein.
exercises.explanation: aufstehen → aufgestanden, with 'sein' as the helper (change of state): 'Ich bin früh aufgestanden.'
